_meta.generated_by: claude (quality-check sample, follows recalibrated AP prompt)
_meta.note: QC batch: real-exam length ~195w, direct-definition opening, insert_text with sentence REMOVED + 4 [■], reference type, single-word vocab options, varied option spread.

items.passage: Chiaroscuro is a painting technique that uses strong contrasts between light and dark to model three-dimensional forms on a flat surface. The term, borrowed from Italian, means literally "light-dark." Renaissance painters developed the method to make figures appear to emerge from shadow, giving their work a dramatic sense of depth. Artists achieved this effect by choosing a single, often unseen light source and carefully grading the tones across each form. [■] Leonardo da Vinci refined the approach into what is sometimes called sfumato, in which edges dissolve into soft, smoky transitions rather than sharp lines. [■] Caravaggio later pushed the contrast to an extreme, plunging much of the canvas into darkness so that a single shaft of light could fall on one gesture or face. [■] The technique was not without its critics. [■] Some viewers complained that such heavy shadow obscured detail and made the paintings hard to read. The method nonetheless became one of the most enduring tools in Western painting.

questions.stem: The word "enduring" in the last sentence is closest in meaning to
questions.explanation: An "enduring" tool is one that has lasted; lasting is the closest synonym.

questions.stem: According to the passage, how did artists produce the chiaroscuro effect?

options.A: By mixing only dark pigments on the canvas
options.B: By choosing one light source and grading the tones
options.C: By copying the work of Leonardo da Vinci

questions.explanation: The passage says artists chose "a single, often unseen light source and carefully grading the tones across each form."

questions.stem: What can be inferred about Caravaggio's paintings compared with earlier ones?

options.A: They used far more dramatic contrast
options.B: They abandoned the use of a light source
options.C: They were easier for viewers to read

questions.explanation: Caravaggio "pushed the contrast to an extreme," implying his paintings used more dramatic contrast than earlier ones.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention sfumato?

options.A: To give an example of how the technique was refined
options.B: To argue that Leonardo rejected chiaroscuro
options.C: To define a new and unrelated movement
options.D: To explain why the technique was criticized

questions.explanation: Sfumato is presented as Leonardo's refinement of the chiaroscuro method.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage where the following sentence could be added. 'For example, in his religious scenes a single beam of light often seems to pick out the most important figure, guiding the viewer's eye.' Where would the sentence best fit?
questions.explanation: The sentence elaborates Caravaggio's use of a single shaft of light, so it follows the Caravaggio sentence at Location 3.

items.passage: Vertical farming is a method of growing crops in stacked layers indoors, often inside repurposed warehouses or shipping containers. Instead of relying on sunlight and soil, these farms use LED lighting and nutrient-rich water to feed the plants. Because the environment is fully controlled, crops can be grown year-round regardless of the weather outside. [■] The approach also dramatically reduces the distance food must travel, since a vertical farm can operate within a city. [■] Supporters argue that this proximity cuts transport emissions and delivers fresher produce to urban markets. [■] The technology does face real obstacles. The artificial lighting consumes a great deal of electricity, and the initial cost of building such a facility is high. [■] For these reasons, vertical farms today tend to focus on leafy greens and herbs, which grow quickly and command high prices, rather than staple crops like wheat or rice.

questions.stem: The word "proximity" in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.explanation: "This proximity" refers to the farm operating within a city, i.e. its closeness to markets.

questions.stem: According to the passage, what do vertical farms use instead of sunlight and soil?
questions.explanation: The passage states the farms "use LED lighting and nutrient-rich water to feed the plants."

questions.stem: What does "this proximity" refer to in the passage?

options.A: A vertical farm operating within a city

questions.explanation: "This proximity" points back to the preceding sentence: a vertical farm operating within a city.

questions.stem: All of the following are mentioned as advantages of vertical farming EXCEPT
questions.explanation: The passage says the lighting "consumes a great deal of electricity" — lower electricity use is NOT claimed; the other three are stated advantages.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage where the following sentence could be added. 'A single building, for instance, can yield several harvests in a season that would normally allow only one.' Where would the sentence best fit?
questions.explanation: The sentence elaborates the year-round, weather-independent growing claim, so it follows that sentence at Location 1.

items.passage: Generational labels such as "Baby Boomer" or "Millennial" are widely used to describe people born within a particular span of years. The labels assume that a shared set of historical events shapes a cohort's attitudes and behavior in lasting ways. Researchers, however, treat these categories with caution. The boundaries between generations are drawn arbitrarily, and the traits assigned to each group are often broad enough to fit almost anyone. A more serious problem concerns cause. When a survey finds that younger people change jobs more frequently, this could be attributed not to generational identity but to life-stage effects, since people of any era tend to move between jobs more often when they are young. Disentangling the two requires data collected over decades. For this reason, many social scientists argue that generational labels are useful for marketing but unreliable as scientific tools.

questions.stem: The word "arbitrarily" in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.explanation: Boundaries drawn "arbitrarily" are drawn without a firm rule, i.e. more or less randomly.

questions.stem: According to the passage, what assumption underlies generational labels?

options.A: That shared historical events lastingly shape a cohort
options.C: That marketing has no measurable effect on consumer behavior
options.D: That life-stage effects explain all attitudes

questions.explanation: The passage says the labels "assume that a shared set of historical events shapes a cohort's attitudes and behavior in lasting ways."

questions.stem: Why does the author mention people changing jobs?

options.A: To illustrate how a life-stage effect can be mistaken for a generational one
options.B: To prove that younger workers are less loyal
options.C: To recommend a particular career strategy
options.D: To show that surveys are always inaccurate

questions.explanation: The job-changing example shows a pattern that life-stage, not generation, may explain — the author's point about confounded causes.

questions.stem: What can be inferred about distinguishing generational effects from life-stage effects?

options.A: It requires data gathered over a long period
options.B: It is essentially impossible under almost any circumstance
options.C: It has already been fully resolved

questions.explanation: The passage states that disentangling the two "requires data collected over decades."

questions.stem: The passage suggests all of the following about generational labels EXCEPT

options.B: Their assigned traits are often very broad
options.D: They can be useful for marketing

questions.explanation: The passage says they are "useful for marketing but unreliable as scientific tools," so calling them reliable scientific tools is the exception.
